perturbation.original_text: By this Agreement the Members enter into a joint venture (the "Venture") in accordance with the laws of the State of Florida. The rights and obligations of the Members will be as stated in the applicable legislation of the State of Florida (the "Act") except as otherwise provided here.
perturbation.changed_text: By this Agreement the Members enter into a joint venture (the "Venture"). The rights and obligations of the Members will generally align with the legislation of the State of Florida (the "Act") but may be subject to interpretations deemed suitable by the Managers.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces ambiguity by replacing the definitive 'in accordance with the laws of the State of Florida' with a weaker statement suggesting general alignment. Adding 'but may be subject to interpretations deemed suitable by the Managers' introduces further uncertainty, allowing managers to potentially deviate from established legal standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 605.04093 outlines the fiduciary duties owed by managers to the LLC. The modification allows managers to deviate from the applicable laws of Florida, which could be seen as an abdication of their fiduciary duty to act in the best interest of the joint venture and its members, potentially prioritizing their own interpretations over legal obligations.

F.S. 605.04093 605.04093 Limitation of liability of managers and members. — (1) A manager in a manager-managed limited liability company or a member in a member-managed limited liability company is not personally liable for monetary damages to the limited liability company, its members, or any other person for any statement, vote, decision, or failure to act regarding management or policy decisions by a manager in a manager-managed limited liability company or a member in a member-managed limited liability company unless: (a) The manager or member breached or failed to perform the duties as a manager in a manager-managed limited liability company or a member in a member-managed limited liability company; and (b) The manager’s or member’s breach of, or failure to perform, those duties constitutes any of the following: 1. A violation of the criminal law unless the manager or member had a reasonable cause to believe his, her, or its conduct was lawful or had no reasonable cause to believe such conduct was unlawful. A judgment or other final adjudication against a manager or member in any criminal proceeding for a violation of the criminal law estops that manager or member from contesting the fact that such breach, or failure to perform, constitutes a violation of the criminal law, but does not estop the manager or member from establishing that he, she, or it had reasonable cause to believe that his, her, or its conduct was lawful or had no reasonable cause to believe that such conduct was unlawful. 2. A transaction from which the manager or member derived an improper personal benefit, directly or indirectly. 3. A distribution in violation of s. 605.0406 . 4. In a proceeding by or in the right of the limited liability company to procure a judgment in its favor or by or in the right of a member, conscious disregard of the best interest of the limited liability company, or willful misconduct. 5. In a proceeding by or in the right of someone other than the limited liability company or a
perturbation.justification: The original text states the joint venture is 'in accordance with the laws of the State of Florida,' creating a clear legal obligation to adhere to Florida law. The modified text weakens this obligation by stating the rights and obligations 'will generally align with the legislation,' and allowing interpretations by managers. This introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing deviations from established legal standards. According to the law explanation, this could be seen as an abdication of their fiduciary duty, prioritizing their interpretations over legal obligations. Scraped law snippet 1 outlines the fiduciary duties of managers, including acting in the best interest of the company. The change increases the potential for managers to act outside of these prescribed legal parameters, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Accurate and complete books of account of the transactions of the Venture will be kept in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) and at all reasonable times will be available and open to inspection and examination by any Member. The books and records of the Venture will reflect all the Venture's transactions and will be appropriate and adequate for the business conducted by the Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: Books of account of the transactions of the Venture will be kept, and will be available for review. The books and records of the Venture will generally reflect the Venture's transactions and will be suitable for the business conducted by the Venture.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for 'accurate and complete' books kept in accordance with GAAP, weakening the standard of accounting. Removing 'at all reasonable times' makes the availability for inspection less certain. Changing 'appropriate and adequate' to 'suitable' introduces further vagueness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Revised Limited Liability Company Act - Record Keeping Requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 605.0407 mandates certain record keeping requirements. By removing the specific requirement to adhere to GAAP and making the availability for inspection less definite, the modification creates an ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance with the statutory requirements for maintaining and providing access to financial records.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXVI > Chapter 605 > Section 0407 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXVI BUSINESS ORGANIZATIONS Chapter 605 FLORIDA REVISED LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 0407 Management of limited liability company. F.S. 605.0407 605.0407 Management of limited liability company. — (1) A limited liability company is a member-managed limited liability company unless the operating agreement or articles of organization: (a) Expressly provide that: 1. The company is or will be manager-managed; 2. The company is or will be managed by managers; or 3. Management of the company is or will be vested in managers; or (b) Include words of similar import to those in subparagraphs (a)1.-3. except that, unless the context in which the expression is used otherwise requires, the terms “managing member” and “managing members” do not, in and of themselves, constitute words of similar import for this purpose. (2) In a member-managed limited liability company, the management and conduct of the company are vested in the members, except as expressly provided in this chapter. (3) In a manager-managed limited liability company, a matter relating to the activities and affairs of the company is decided exclusively by the manager, or if there is more than one manager, by the managers, except as expressly provided in this chapter. (4) A member is not entitled to remuneration for services performed for a member-managed limited liability company, except for reasonable compensation for services rendered in winding up the activities and affairs of the company, in the absence of an agreement to the contrary. (5) A limited liability company shall reimburse a member for an advance to the company beyond the amount of capital the member agreed to contribute. (6) The dissolution of a limited liability company does not affect the applicability of this section and ss. 605.04071 - 605.04074 . However, a person who wrongfully causes dissolution of the company loses the right to participate in management as a member and a manager. History. — s. 2, ch. 2013-180.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'accurate and complete' books of account according to GAAP, available for inspection 'at all reasonable times,' and 'appropriate and adequate' records. The modified text weakens these requirements by removing GAAP adherence, making inspection availability less certain, and using the vaguer term 'suitable.' This creates ambiguity regarding the standard of accounting and access to records. While Florida Statute 605.0407 primarily addresses the management structure of LLCs, it implies the necessity of proper record-keeping by stating that 'the management and conduct of the company are vested in the members' (subsection 2) or managers (subsection 3). By weakening the standards for financial record-keeping and access, the change introduces ambiguity that could impede effective management and oversight, thus contradicting the implicit requirement for proper records.

perturbation.original_text: All meetings will be held at a time and in a location that is reasonable, convenient and practical considering the situation of all Members.
perturbation.changed_text: Meetings will be held at times and locations that are considered appropriate by the Managers.
perturbation.explanation: This replaces the objective standard of 'reasonable, convenient and practical considering the situation of all Members' with the subjective assessment of 'considered appropriate by the Managers.' This allows managers to potentially prioritize their own convenience over that of other members, creating ambiguity and potential for abuse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Revised Limited Liability Company Act - Member's Right to Information
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 605.0410(2)(b) grants each member the right to participate in management. By allowing the managers to solely determine when and where meetings take place, there is a potential for meetings to be scheduled in such a way that some members are effectively excluded from participating, thereby infringing upon their statutory right to manage.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates meetings be held at a 'reasonable, convenient and practical' time and location, considering all members' situations. This objective standard is replaced with the subjective assessment of the managers, giving them sole discretion. The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute 605.0410(2)(b) grants each member the right to participate in management. By allowing managers to solely determine meeting times and locations, there is a high potential for members to be excluded, infringing on their statutory right to manage. The changed text creates ambiguity and potential for abuse, contradicting the members' right to participate, creating a legally ambiguous obligation.
